name: El Shaddai
body: God introduced himself to Abram by this name just before renaming him Abraham, promising to make an old, childless man the father of nations. It is a name that shows up when the odds look impossible.

name: El Elyon
body: Melchizedek, the mysterious priest-king of Salem, blessed Abram in this name after his rescue of Lot — the first time in Scripture God is called Most High, possessor of heaven and earth.

name: El Roi
body: Hagar, alone and pregnant in the wilderness after being cast out, gave God this name — the only time in the Bible a person names God. She named him for noticing her when no one else did.

name: Elohim
body: The very first word used for God in Scripture, a plural form of majesty used with singular verbs. It opens the Bible over creation itself, before any covenant name is given.

name: Yahweh-Jireh
body: Abraham named the mountain this after God supplied a ram in place of Isaac at the last possible moment. The name became proverbial: on Yahweh’s mountain, it will be provided.

name: Yahweh-Rapha
body: Just after the Israelites left Egypt, God revealed himself by this name at Marah, promising to keep them free of the diseases they had known there if they listened to him.

name: Yahweh-Nissi
body: After Israel’s victory over Amalek, Moses built an altar and gave it this name, marking Yahweh as the rallying standard under which the battle had actually been won.

name: Yahweh-Shalom
body: Gideon built an altar by this name after an angel of Yahweh met him and he lived to tell about it, expecting to die for seeing God face to face. Peace, not judgment, met him instead.

name: Yahweh-Raah
body: David, once a shepherd himself, opens his most beloved psalm with this confession — Yahweh doing for him what he once did for sheep: leading, feeding, and guarding.

name: Yahweh-Tsidkenu
body: Jeremiah gives this as the name of the coming righteous Branch of David, a king who would succeed where Judah’s failed kings had not, becoming the very righteousness his people lacked.

name: Immanuel
body: First given to Isaiah as a sign to a frightened king, this name is picked up centuries later by Matthew to describe the birth of Jesus — the promise that God had not stayed distant after all.

name: Alpha and Omega
body: The first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, used three times in Revelation as a title for God and for Jesus — everything that exists falls somewhere between these two letters.

name: I Am
body: God’s answer to Moses at the burning bush when asked for a name — a declaration of pure, self-sustaining existence. Centuries later, Jesus applied the same words to himself before a stunned crowd.

name: Lamb of God
meaning: The sacrificial Lamb provided by God
body: John the Baptist used this title the moment he saw Jesus approaching, pointing to a sacrifice that would finally take away sin rather than merely cover it for another year.

name: Lion of Judah
original: Ὁ λέων ὁ ἐκ τῆς φυλῆς Ἰούδα
meaning: The Lion of the tribe of Judah
body: In John’s vision of heaven, an elder announces this title to explain who alone is worthy to open the sealed scroll — strength and kingship, tracing back to Jacob’s ancient blessing over Judah.

name: Wonderful Counselor
body: One of four titles Isaiah gives the promised child whose government would never end — wisdom beyond ordinary counsel, wonderful in the sense of astonishing, beyond expectation.

name: Mighty God
body: The second of Isaiah’s four titles for the coming child, placing raw divine power alongside the tenderness of a newborn — a paradox the prophet leaves unresolved on the page.

name: Everlasting Father
body: The third of Isaiah’s titles, an unusual pairing of fatherly care with an eternity no human father could offer — closer to protector of ages than the modern sense of a parent.

name: Prince of Peace
body: Isaiah’s fourth title for the coming king, promising an end to the endless wars of his day through a ruler whose peace would keep expanding rather than being negotiated away.

name: Bread of Life
body: Jesus used this title the day after feeding a crowd with a few loaves, telling them that the true bread they needed wasn’t physical at all — it was himself, freely offered.

name: Light of the World
body: Spoken during the Feast of Tabernacles, when huge lamps lit the temple courts each night, Jesus claimed to be a light that his followers would carry with them long after the festival lamps went out.

name: Good Shepherd
body: Jesus contrasts himself with hired hands who flee when danger comes, describing a shepherd who knows each sheep by name and is willing to die to protect the flock.

name: The Word
body: John opens his Gospel with this title, echoing Genesis’s opening line to declare that the one who would become flesh had already existed with God, and as God, before anything was made.

name: Ancient of Days
body: In Daniel’s night vision, thrones are set and one whose clothing is white as snow takes his seat — an image of timeless authority, presiding over kingdoms that rise and fall beneath him — a title from the Aramaic chapters of Daniel.

name: The Rock
body: Moses uses this title in his closing song over Israel, and Isaiah later calls Yahweh an everlasting Rock — an image of a foundation that does not shift no matter what else moves around it.

name: King of Kings
meaning: King of Kings and Lord of Lords
body: Written on the robe and thigh of the rider on the white horse in Revelation’s vision, and used by Paul of the one true Ruler — a title that outranks every throne that has ever claimed authority.

name: Chief Cornerstone
body: Peter and Paul both apply this image to Jesus, quoting an old psalm about a stone the builders rejected — the one piece that ends up setting the alignment for an entire structure.

name: Bright and Morning Star
original: Ὁ ἀστὴρ ὁ λαμπρὸς ὁ πρωϊνός
body: Near the very end of Revelation, Jesus gives himself this title — the star that signals night is ending, appearing right before the first light of an unending morning.
